control_description: The entity implements logical access security measures to protect against threats from sources outside its system boundaries.

evidence_files.description: Meridian SaaS system boundary and access control policy requiring all traffic to traverse CloudFront, WAF, Kong API Gateway, and internal ALB
Network architecture document showing standard traffic flow and the admin tool's direct bypass path
Four days of admin tool access logs showing user actions, source IPs, and operations performed
Q3 2025 external penetration test report covering all endpoints including the admin tool

prompt: You are a SOC 2 auditor evaluating Meridian SaaS' system boundary controls for the Q4 2025 observation period. Review the boundary policy, network architecture, admin tool access logs, and penetration test report. Assess whether CC6.6 requirements for system boundary protection are met. Consider whether any boundary bypasses are adequately controlled, whether compensating controls are sufficient, and whether the overall boundary architecture is effective. Apply professional judgment to determine materiality.

findings.title: Admin tool bypasses WAF and API Gateway with no remaining technical blocker for migration
findings.description: The admin tool (admin.internal.meridian.io:8443) bypasses CloudFront, WAF, and Kong API Gateway, routing directly from VPN to internal ALB. Policy Section 3.1 states 'No production service shall accept traffic that bypasses any layer of this boundary stack,' and Section 3.2 confirms internal services are subject to the same requirements. Exception SEC-EXC-2024-018 was filed in March 2024, and the original technical justification (Kong did not support WebSocket) was resolved when Kong 3.4 added WebSocket support in August 2024 — over 16 months ago. The pentest (TV-MRD-2025-001) confirms the bypass and recommends migration. Compensating controls exist (VPN, SSO, MFA, RBAC, audit logging), but the admin tool handles highly sensitive operations: customer suspensions, data exports (including legal holds and GDPR requests), feature flag changes, circuit breaker management, and scaling parameters. The judgment call: the compensating controls provide strong access restriction, but the tool processes the most sensitive operations in the platform without WAF payload inspection, Kong schema validation, or Kong anomaly detection. The original technical blocker no longer exists, suggesting this is now an organizational prioritization issue rather than a technical constraint.
findings.expected_reasoning: The auditor should trace the admin tool's bypass path, verify it violates Section 3.1, check the exception, note that the technical blocker (WebSocket support) was resolved 16 months ago, and weigh the compensating controls against the sensitivity of the operations. The VPN + SSO + MFA + RBAC provide strong authentication and authorization, but the absence of WAF and Kong means no payload inspection, no schema validation, and no centralized anomaly detection for the most privileged operations. At 0.03% of traffic, volume-based materiality suggests low risk, but operation-based materiality (customer data exports, circuit breakers) suggests high risk.

findings.title: Admin tool audit logs not forwarded to SIEM for correlation and anomaly detection
findings.description: The penetration test finding TV-MRD-2025-003 identifies that admin tool actions are logged to CloudWatch but not forwarded to Splunk SIEM. Policy Section 5.1 states 'Kong API Gateway logs forwarded to Splunk with 365-day retention' as the standard for audit logging. Because the admin tool bypasses Kong, it also bypasses Kong's centralized audit logging and anomaly detection. The admin-specific CloudWatch logs capture actions but are not correlated with other security events or subject to automated anomaly detection. Remediation is planned for Q1 2026 but not yet implemented. The admin access logs show sensitive operations including customer data exports for legal holds and GDPR requests, customer suspensions, and infrastructure changes (circuit breakers, scaling) — all without SIEM visibility.
findings.expected_reasoning: The auditor should connect the admin tool bypass to the absence of SIEM integration. Because the admin tool bypasses Kong, it also misses Kong's centralized audit logging. The CloudWatch logs exist but are siloed. The judgment call: the logs exist and are retained for 365 days, so there is an audit trail, but without SIEM integration, anomalous admin behavior (e.g., unusual data export patterns, bulk customer suspensions) would not trigger automated alerts.

findings.title: Service account and internal cron accessing admin tool from non-VPN IP
findings.description: The admin access logs show a request from IP 10.0.4.15 (internal-cron@meridian.io) on December 18, performing a 'cleanup_expired_sessions' action. This IP (10.0.4.15) is in the production VPC CIDR range, not the Tailscale VPN CIDR (100.64.0.0/10). Section 3.2 of the boundary policy requires VPN connectivity for all internal service access. While this appears to be an internal cron job performing maintenance, it accesses the admin tool from a non-VPN path, which violates the policy. Additionally, admin-svc@meridian.io performs customer data exports — this service account accesses the admin tool via VPN (100.64.45.22) but the nature of automated service accounts performing customer data exports warrants scrutiny regarding whether these exports are individually authorized.
findings.expected_reasoning: The auditor should review the access logs for source IPs and note that 10.0.4.15 is not in the VPN CIDR. This suggests the admin tool is accessible from the production VPC without VPN, which is a boundary control weakness. The judgment call: this may be an intentional cron job configuration, but it violates the VPN requirement and suggests the admin tool's network controls are not as strict as documented.
